posts.text:
Each year, millions of antelope traverse South Sudan, moving toward permanent water and greener grass during the dry season and spreading out across tens of thousands of square miles when the rains return. For decades, the largest land mammal migration on Earth was masked by the brutal conflict in the region, but the relative peace of recent years has brought new pressures, as people looking to restart their lives are expanding settlements. Nat Geo photographer Marcus Westberg @marcuswestbergphotography has filmed this massive migration by drone, and his groundbreaking footage shows the immense scale of this natural wonder.

Learn more about current threats to the migration and the people who are trying to protect it at the link in bio.

Sperm whales, like this adult and calf swimming near Dominica and captured by @brianskerry, “speak” in clicks, which they make in rhythmic series called codas. In what may be the largest interspecies communication effort in history, scientists are using machine learning to try to decode what these animals say to one another.

Dive deeper into the language of sperm whales at the link in bio.

Is there anything more relaxing than the sound of a waterfall? 😌

#FiennesReturnToTheWild is now streaming on @DisneyPlus.

Unwind with a little assistance from some underwater coral. 🪸

#AmericasNationalParks is streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

The Arctic tundra blooms briefly each summer, carpeting the landscape along Hudson Bay with pink wildflowers. As a rainbow stretched across the sky, Martin Gregus Jr. @mywildlive captured this polar bear roaming the area, waiting for sea ice to form when winter returns.

In this image by Nat Geo photographer @thomaspeschak from our January 2026 issue, meerkats stand sentinel in South Africa’s Tswalu Kalahari Reserve, where they live in tightly knit clans led by a dominant female. This matriarch—often the only one to breed—keeps the group fed, protected, and in line, with helpers raising pups, foraging, and standing guard.

See more Photos of the Day at the link in bio.

In this image from our photography archives, a praying mantis stretches her forelegs beneath mushrooms in Bulgaria’s Sredna Gora mountains. Photographer @georgi_georgiev_photography, a trained ecologist, has observed these ambush predators hunt creatures as large as a lizard, but also pause to carefully clean their forelegs and antennae. “The smaller they are, the more hidden and interesting their world is to me."

See more Photos of the Day at the link in bio.

Turn up the volume and excuse the pollen—this bee is hard at work, moving from bloom to bloom. 🐝

#ARealBugsLife is now streaming on @disneyplus.

Humans often depict our closest relatives, Homo neanderthalensis, as club-wielding brutes but recent evidence suggests they were cerebral creatures capable of artistic expression and honoring their dead.

Now, a new discovery suggests that Neanderthals also displayed a deft touch when treating dental pain. A team of researchers recently examined a peculiar hole inside a nearly 60,000-year-old Neanderthal molar found in a Siberian cave. Their findings suggest that a tool-wielding Neanderthal drilled into the tooth to carefully remove its damaged core. The prehistoric procedure predates the earliest evidence of modern humans drilling into cavities by some 40,000 years.

Head to the link in bio to uncover more about Neanderthal ‘dentists’.

Image courtesy of Zubova et al., 2026, PLOS One, CC-BY 4.0

Photos by @joelsartore | Vulturine guineafowl are named for their bald head and neck, which resemble a vulture's. They may look placid, but these birds can be quite aggressive toward one another, especially during periods when food or roosting areas are scarce. Such aggression isn’t limited to adults. Chicks have also been known to attack each other. Photos taken @lincolnchildrenszoo. To see more species featured in the Photo Ark, follow me, @joelsartore. #PhotoArk

For our May issue, Nat Geo photographer @marcuswestbergphotography filmed the world's largest land mammal migration, in which millions of antelope cross South Sudan. Getting close to an event of this scale is no mean feat and requires some clever positioning. To capture this 360 shot, he and his team hid in the grass, all while battling overheating cameras and horseflies.

Join him inside the field and learn what it took to document six million antelope on the move at the link in bio.

For centuries, a fabled Incan fortress called Ancocogua existed only in legend. Now, researchers think they’ve finally found the long-rumored lost citadel—and new clues about the empire’s last days.

In the cover story of our June issue, out now, we venture high into the Peruvian Andes to show how the discovery of a trove of gold and silver sequins—found in what had been an alpaca corral—could help resolve this ancient Incan mystery. Read the article by Alejandro Muñoz @alexmunofer at the link in our bio, featuring photographs by Arturo Rodríguez @arturorguezdotcom.

Also in this issue, we explore inside the epic construction of the world’s tallest church, Barcelona’s Sagrada Familia basilica—an architectural marvel 144 years in the making. We head deep into the Alaskan backcountry with a renegade geologist attempting to predict catastrophic landslides. And we come face-to-face with India’s last lions, now pushing the boundaries of their habitat.

Baby orangutans spend years clinging to their mothers, learning what to eat and how to survive in the rainforest. Even snacktime is a lesson. Filmed by Nat Geo photographer and filmmaker @timlaman in Borneo’s Gunung Palung National Park—where National Geographic Explorer Cheryl Knott has studied wild orangutans for more than three decades—this moment captures the messy, fuzzy reality of growing up as an orangutan.

There are millions of plant and animal species on our planet, but only three rock types: igneous, metamorphic, and sedimentary. They're all around us—where do they come from and how do they form? 🪨

Become a backyard geologist with our Wonder Lessons to learn all about rocks, starting with how they form at the link in bio.

Photograph by Will Matsuda, National Geographic

Warning: Don’t watch if you’re hungry!

A new season of #TucciInItaly is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.
They make up around half of the world’s wild tiger population, but there's still a lot that people don’t know about Bengal tigers. Discover five facts about these wild-for-water felines.

On a cold, crisp winter morning at Keoladeo Bird Sanctuary in Bharatpur, Rajasthan, India, three spotted owlets sat pressed together on a tree perch, turning companionship into a survival strategy. Photographed on January 29, 2026, during a birding expedition with fellow photographers, the forest felt hushed, as if pausing to witness their quiet bond. In that shared stillness, the owlets seemed to whisper an old winter story of warmth and trust.

This photo by @navile.shroff.photography is from our @natgeoyourshot community.

A tardigrade is difficult to see without magnification, yet it lives in moss growing in everyday places. With a soaked sample and a microscope, you can observe these tiny—but giant in their own world—animals crawl around. Nat Geo Explorer @arielwaldman shares more fascinating details about these microwildlife species.

There's nothing like motherly love, whether among humans, Asiatic lions, or any other species 💛

Want to learn more about the last Asiatic lions and the efforts to save them? Head to the link in bio.

Video by Nimit Pandya (@nimit654)

A wolf pack takes on an elk five times their size—but strength alone won’t be enough to succeed.

#WildYellowstone is now streaming on @DisneyPlus.

When lava meets the ocean, two great forces collide—and the results are nothing short of explosive. 

#WildHawaii is streaming on @DisneyPlus.

Photos by @joelsartore | Seeing a spotted bat in person is a very rare thing, so when one finally came into human care recently at Arizona Bat Rescue, I jumped at the chance to add it to the Photo Ark. Small and secretive, they're said to have the largest ears of any North American bat species—up to an inch and a half!

Arizona Bat Rescue's teams do amazing work in helping dozens of injured and orphaned bats in the state return to the wild each year. They know that bats are not only smart and personable but also very helpful with pollination and natural insect control. Photos taken @azbatrescue. To see more species featured in the Photo Ark, follow me @joelsartore. #PhotoArk

South Sudan is covered in vast and fertile floodplains, savannas, and grasslands. But due to decades of brutal conflict, the country’s natural wonders have largely remained a secret to the outside world.

A short peace agreement in 2007 finally gave scientists a window to conduct a brief aerial survey of the landscape. What they found was extraordinary: seemingly endless herds of animals traversing the grasslands in an unbroken stream. Each year, millions of antelope move in a sweeping circuit across southeastern South Sudan.

More recent aerial counts conducted by the conservation nonprofit @africanparksnetwork indicate the population approaches six million—the largest land mammal migration on Earth. Today the relative peace in the region has brought new pressures, as people looking to restart their lives are expanding settlements, hunting bushmeat to feed their families and, in some places, weighing whether oil extraction might be a path to prosperity.

At this critical moment, an emerging corps of guardians is finding ways to keep the migration alive and provide its wildlife refuge in places like @boma_badingilo_southsudan.

Learn more at the link in bio.

Photos by @marcuswestbergphotography

These adorable nibbles are more than just affection. Grooming strengthens social bonds among meerkat clan members, which is vital to keeping the group safe from the numerous predators they face in the Kalahari Desert—and even rival meerkats.

Learn more about life in the meerkat world at the link in bio. 

Video by @ottowhitehead

Alzheimer’s disease symptoms and other forms of dementia don't occur overnight. Up to a decade before memory impairment appears, subtle cognitive symptoms can emerge. While these signs alone aren't definitive, recognizing patterns can help patients and families prepare for what lies ahead.

Learn more about these signs and what experts say patients and their families can do at the link in bio.

Photograph by Jason Edwards (@jasonedwardsng)

Wishing a Happy Birthday to Sir David Attenborough. Thank you for the knowledge, passion, and hope you’ve passed on to all of us.

Celebrate 100 years of Sir David Attenborough with Ocean with David Attenborough on @DisneyPlus and @hulu

Spring bloom ASMR? Don't mind if we do. 💐

#AmericasNationalParks is now streaming on @DisneyPlus

Lion cub roars are unmatched 🦁💛 These little cubs are just getting started, and as they grow, so will their roar. For males, that mighty sound could one day travel up to five miles across sub-Saharan Africa. 

#NatGeoQueens is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

A sense of wonder comes from curiosity. For director and National Geographic Explorer-at-Large @jamescameronofficial, that's the driving force behind the "Secrets Of" series-because by shedding light on the worlds of bees, elephants, and more, we can truly appreciate the importance of these animals.

Stream #SecretsOfTheBees now on @DisneyPlus and @hulu, and get ready for #SecretsOfTheBears, coming soon!

On May 8 Sir David Attenborough celebrates his 100th birthday, but he is receiving an unusual early present—a wasp species named after him. 

This wasp species has been known for decades, but was only recently designated by Gavin Broad, wasp taxonomist and Principal Curator, Insects, at the Natural History Museum, London. Named Attenboroughnculus tau, Broad wanted to honor the man who introduced him to the concept of "taxonomy" when he was young. As a result, this tiny species, which devours other insects alive, is one of over 50 other species named in Attenborough's honor so far—including an echidna, a fungus, and an extinct marine reptile. 

Discover more about this new wasp species and others named after Sir David Attenborough at the link in bio. 

Photograph courtesy Trustees of the Natural History Museum

Presented by National Geographic Society in partnership with Rolex. 

Conservation biologist Krithi Karanth is the 2026 Rolex National Geographic Explorer of the Year.

In India, when wildlife damages property or causes a loss of life, it can lead to retributive hunting—destroying the bonds between humans and nature. As the CEO of the Bengaluru-based Centre for Wildlife Studies, Karanth has spent years finding ways to help people live peacefully alongside these animals. Through initiatives that help locals more easily request government remuneration for wildlife-related damage and education programs for those living closest to reserves, Karanth combines science, education, and community partnerships to redefine how people coexist with nature. 

Since 2011, the Rolex National Geographic Explorer of the Year Award has recognized Explorers shining a light on important issues, discoveries, and challenges facing our planet to generate a sustainable future. Discover more about Karanth’s work at the link in bio. #PerpetualPlanet

Photographs by Rebecca Hale (@beckythale) and @alishavasudev

In his hometown of Osun, Nigeria, photographer David Olayide (@olayidedavid) posed two of his siblings for a portrait meant to make a statement about what we choose to keep and to dispose of. He dressed Charles (at left) and Zipporah Olayide in masks made from discarded VHS tapes, along with traditional Yoruba garments their parents wore during their wedding ceremony. It’s not the first time he’s enlisted his brother and sister to help him execute his artistic vision. “After a while they begin to just settle into the whole idea,” Olayide says, “and they usually are very cool.”

Meet Cinco. A remote camera in southern Arizona has caught a rare glimpse of a jaguar, one of only five documented in the region over the past 15 years. His presence reflects a gradual return of the Western Hemisphere's largest cat across the U.S.-Mexico border after decades of decline due to hunting and habitat loss. The footage also reveals how these elusive cats move through the landscape, but with no breeding females confirmed and growing barriers to their range, that foothold remains uncertain.

Find out what Cinco's appearance could signal for the species at the link in bio.

Video by University of Arizona Wild Cat Research & Conservation Center

Trees are all around us, so their details can be easy to miss. But learning to read clues in leaves, bark, and other features can help you identify common species hiding in plain sight. 🌳

Learn more about identifying trees, starting with the mighty oak at the link in bio.

Photograph by Will Matsuda

Video by @awais.ali.sheikh | An Atlantic puffin gathering grass for its nest suddenly dropped everything after spotting its partner flying overhead. It instinctively launched into a joyful courtship ritual by tossing the grass aside and sharply lifting its head and beak toward the sky. This tender moment unfolded at Látrabjarg, Iceland, one of Europe’s highest sea cliffs and a vital breeding ground where puffins return year after year.

This video was featured in our @natgeoyourshot #NatGeoYourShotPOY25 challenge.

A massive sea lion nicknamed “Chonkers” is having his moment at Pier 39 in San Francisco, drawing crowds eager to take in his sheer size. Chonkers is not a California sea lion, the species people are used to seeing along the coast. Instead, he’s a Steller sea lion, a species that ranges along the North Pacific Rim. While the two look similar, there are key differences—most notably size, with Steller sea lions growing over twice the size of California sea lions. While "Chonkers" isn't the first of its kind to appear at Pier 39, the length of his stay is rare.

Learn more at the link in bio.

Photograph by Jomel Loresca

Photos by @joelsartore | Napo sakis are known for their coarse, wiry body hair. It performs what is known as piloerection, or the involuntary standing of body hair caused by the contraction of tiny muscles. In warmer months, the fluffing of Napo sakis' hair increases air circulation over the skin, helping dissipate heat. When it's colder, raised hair follicles trap air close to the skin, creating insulation that prevents heat loss. Photos taken @rarec.center. To see more species featured in the Photo Ark, follow me @joelsartore. #PhotoArk

Millions of people across the United States suffer from depression, but treatment can often be costly, ineffective, and frustrating. To improve this, researchers have used brain imaging to identify six distinct "biotypes" of depression, hoping to one day provide targeted treatment based on an individual's unique biomarkers.

Learn more at the link in bio.

Photograph by @beckythale

By tapping into the beliefs of the Egyptian people and channeling theological authority, Cleopatra legitimized both her reign and her descendants. 

#LostTreasuresOfEgypt is streaming on @DisneyPlus.

Along the coast of Hudson Bay, polar bear cubs depend entirely on their mother for warmth, protection, and nourishment in their first years. As the sun rises, the cubs wake, stretch, and press close to nurse while she scans her surroundings. Captured by Martin Gregus Jr. @mywildlive, this moment shows how even at rest, a mother polar bear remains watchful over her cubs.

Photos by Muhammed Muheisen @mmuheisen | Each year I look forward to capturing these moments in time as the tulip fields come into bloom. From above they form colorful carpets, and the air carries the scent of flowers.

On April 20 and 23, I photographed these scenes while flying over the fields of the Dutch provinces of South Holland and Flevoland. They are part of a story I have been working on for several years, documenting flower season in the Netherlands. For more photos and videos from around the world, follow @mmuheisen.

Colorectal cancer is rising among younger adults—and for years, the reasons behind the trend were unclear. A 2025 study reveals a surprising potential link—colibactin, a toxin produced by E. coli and other bacteria that can damage DNA. 

Find out how this finding could change the future of colon cancer prevention at the link in bio.

Micrograph by Steve Gschmeissner, Science Photo Library

Ancient bald cypress trees record centuries of drought and rainfall in the rings beneath their bark, growing across freshwater swamps as captured by National Geographic Explorer @macstonephoto. These trees, some of the most resilient on Earth, also teach scientists lessons about our climate future and how to save other wetlands—even as saltwater pushes farther into their own, thinning the forests.

Head to the link in bio to learn how coastal forests are changing along the U.S. shoreline.

What actually happens when you stop eating meat—even for just a short time? Research suggests the changes begin faster than you might expect, from shifts in gut bacteria to improvements in cholesterol and heart health. But the real story isn’t just what disappears from your plate—it’s what takes its place.

Find out what the science says—and what it means for your next meal—at the link in bio.

Photograph by @andrea_frazzeta

National Geographic Explorer and Photo Ark founder @joelsartore has photographed thousands of animals in his quest to document at-risk species. What does someone who's seen so many of Earth's incredible creatures find wondrous? People who devote their time to helping those creatures survive.

Join Explorers and #StepIntoWonder with National Geographic’s #EarthMonth collection throughout April on @DisneyPlus.

Flowers may look peaceful, but if you really pay attention to them, you may find a busy secret world. 🌸 🐝

Spot more of the #SecretsOfTheBees, now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu

Around the globe, a growing cadre of bee researchers is trying to measure and understand how bees change their behaviors to solve and survive new challenges. And that means dreaming up ever more outlandish obstacle courses. In one case, researchers in Finland are seeing if bumblebees can use a ball as a tool to reach a sugary reward. The results are helping deepen our understanding of insect intelligence.

Learn more about the sneaky genius of nature's brightest thinkers at the link in bio.

#SecretsOfTheBees is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

Video by Reuben Wu @reuben | At Abraham Lake in Alberta, Canada, I walk across clear winter ice above layers of methane bubbles trapped beneath the surface. They're formed by the decomposing organic matter at the bottom of the lake.

Each step became its own sound, the sharp crunch of spikes on ice that felt both solid and precarious.

The ice here was about 12 inches (30 centimeters) thick, but that measure is no guarantee of safety. I still had to move carefully and stay alert for weaker sections that could give way without warning.

For more visual stories, follow me @reuben.

Venomous, well-armored, and extreme binge eaters, Gila monsters are rarely seen but incredibly remarkable reptiles whose venom even has medicinal applications. Discover five surprising facts about Gila monsters.

Photo by @beverlyjoubert | Watching this elephant in Zimbabwe’s Sapi Reserve, situated on the edges of the Zambezi River, the connection to water is unmistakable. And this is no coincidence. The ancestors of today's elephants once moved through swamps and river margins, creatures of wetter worlds. Even now, there are traces of that history: in the way elephants swim with ease, in their instinctive pull toward water, in how their lives are shaped by it. To survive, an elephant must drink hundreds of liters a day, and the animals can lose vast amounts (up to 10% of all the water in their bodies in a single day) when the heat grows intense. As rising temperatures and changing rainfall patterns make water less certain all across Africa, elephants—with their sheer size and daily water demands—are especially vulnerable. 

To see more of my work, follow me @beverlyjoubert.

Could they bee any cuter? 🐝

Make a bee-line to #SecretsOfTheBees on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

There are forces of nature… and forces of wonder.
 
#StepIntoWonder with National Geographic’s #EarthMonth collection on @DisneyPlus and don’t miss Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu in theaters and IMAX May 22.

This image was taken near Polán, Toledo province (Spain) last August (2025), from a tiny hide-out overlooking a small waterhole where lynxes occasionally come down to drink. It was an extremely hot afternoon, and a rabbit was very close to the water.

Suddenly, a lynx appeared silently, but the rabbit noticed it at the very last second. In a desperate attempt to escape, it launched itself into the water and then made an incredible leap over the lynx’s paw. I couldn’t believe what I was witnessing. For a brief moment, the rabbit actually escaped, and then the chase began. It lasted only a couple of minutes, but it felt endless.

I’ve been photographing Iberian lynxes for more than five years, and this was the first time in my life I was able to witness and document a complete hunting sequence.

This photo by @alexandrovich_yo is from our @natgeoyourshot community.

While storm chasing in Texas’s Big Bend National Park, photographer @paulmsmithphotography captured what scientists call a transient luminous event, or TLE, a brief electrical flash above storm clouds that can last less than a millisecond. This one unfurls as a jellyfish sprite. Seeking them out, Smith says, is “like deep-sea fishing in the upper atmosphere—you don’t know what you’re going to get.”

Clouds are easy to overlook—but they're always changing, shaped by the air around us. ☁️ Catch up on previous Wonder Lessons about clouds at the link in bio.

For more Wonder Lessons, be sure to follow along on @natgeohistory's Facebook and Instagram Stories all week long, where we'll be learning about rocks. 🪨

Photograph by @willfujiomatsuda

@bertiegregory is buzzing to show off his bee pun skills. Don't swarm him if he missed a few—we know it must sting.

Drop your best bee puns in the comments and bee-come an even better bee punner by watching #SecretsOfTheBees, now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

Serving looks, protecting their young, and risking it all for love—@nikkiglaser learns that nature will always be the main character.

Stream these National Geographic shows now on @DisneyPlus, and don't miss Nikki Glaser's stand-up special Good Girl, streaming now on @hulu.

Allison Fong's (@808brazenbites) moment of wonder? Making the journey to the North Pole—and getting to see firsthand just what lives in the sea ice. 

#StepIntoWonder with National Geographic’s #EarthMonth collection throughout April and stream #PoleToPole with Will Smith on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

“Frozen shoulder” is a real condition causing stiffness and difficulty in raising the arm. It mostly affects women, striking peri- or post-menopausal women more commonly than anyone else. The painful affliction can last for years—but has long been ignored by medical researchers who doubted its existence. New treatments may finally promise a cure.

Learn more about frozen shoulder and how it might finally be addressed at the link in bio.

Photograph by K H Fung, Science Photo Library

Drop by drop, winter is on its way out, and the spring blooms aren't far behind. 💐

#IncredibleAnimalJourneys is now streaming on @DisneyPlus

Video by @joelsartore | As their name suggests, Chinese water dragons spend most of their time near a water source, taking up residence close to riverbanks, in rainforests, or in swampy areas. Strong swimmers, they are able to take refuge in the water when they are nervous or frightened. If need be, they can hide from predators by submerging themselves underwater for up to 25 minutes! This video was taken @lincolnchildrenszoo. To see more species featured in the Photo Ark, follow me, @joelsartore. #PhotoArk

Our planet is a wondrous solar powerhouse 🌞✨ For billions of years, plants have been capturing sunlight and turning it into life—nature’s own incredible energy magic! #StepIntoWonder

#OneStrangeRock is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

Drifting on Lake Kerkini, Central Macedonia, Greece, under a sky that promised nothing… until it did. For a few fragile minutes, the clouds parted, painting the water in pink and purple, and a Dalmatian pelican slid into perfect light. No time to think—just instinct and a click. A split second where doubt turned into one of my favorite frames I’ve ever captured.

This photo by @tony_wanders_photo is from our @natgeoyourshot community.

Polar bears mostly stick to Arctic ice, but they can swim well and often enter nearby coastal waters to hunt or travel. In this moment, captured by Martin Gregus Jr. (@mywildlive), the belugas surrounding the mammal are in luck as the lone swimmer is in no position to attack.

Canada’s last free-ranging reindeer herd is on the move—and so is its future. Intended to replace dwindling caribou populations, thousands of reindeer were driven to Canada in the early 20th Century by way of Alaska. This century-old herd is now formally owned by Inuvialuit herders looking to create a sustainable path to food security and self-reliance on their ancestral land. 

Read more about the Canadian Reindeer Project at the link in bio.

Photos @katieorlinsky

For National Geographic Fellow @sandesh_kadur, the natural world has always been a source of wonder—especially when it comes to big cats. It’s those rare, almost surreal moments in the wild that stay with him. 

Join Explorers and #StepIntoWonder with National Geographic’s #EarthMonth collection throughout April on @DisneyPlus.

For as long as she’s been touring, @billieeilish has been shocked to see how environmentally destructive the concert business is. “I didn't know how bad it could be,” Eilish says. “The waste involved in live music is really hard to take in.” 

Now, as one of the world’s biggest pop stars, she is building a blueprint for change. On her most recent tour, Eilish offered reusable water bottles, sold merch made from recycled materials, and adorned venues with signage displaying statistics emphasizing how our everyday behaviors can impact the climate. 

The efforts were a small piece of a larger environmental initiative to make touring less terrible for the planet. The results, outlined in a recent impact report, represent an unprecedented milestone that Eilish hopes can be a model for other artists. 

Learn more about how Eilish is influencing sustainability practices in the music industry—and for her fans—at the link in bio. 

Photos by @austinhargrave 
Video by David Dalasta (@fivesixpro)

Every year, monarch butterflies embark on a five-month journey from Mexico to Canada and back again—a multi-generational migration that's extremely rare in nature. 🦋

#GreatMigrations is streaming on @DisneyPlus

Kea parrots depend heavily on their beaks for everything from foraging to preening, so missing a part of it would typically put a bird at a serious disadvantage. But at a wildlife reserve in New Zealand, one precocious parrot—Bruce—is defying the odds. With only a half-beak, he’s developed a novel jousting-like attack with his feet and lower beak to vanquish rivals and achieve alpha male status in his circus.
 
Learn more about Bruce’s ingenious combat skills at the link in bio
 
Video by Alex Grabham

There are around 20,000 bees out there, so how many can @bertiegregory name in just 30 seconds?

Bee sure to stream #SecretsOfTheBees on @DisneyPlus and @hulu if you want to improve your knowledge!

Possibly the cutest close-up you’ve seen today. Whether they're digging burrows, foraging, or exercising their curiosity, meerkats rely on their keen senses every day to navigate environments like the Kalahari Desert. 

Video by @ottowhitehead

On clear nights, you can see the moon, planets, stars, and constellations in the sky. 

One of the best-known shapes in the sky is the Big Dipper—a group of stars in the Northern Hemisphere that resembles a kitchen ladle. Locating this Northern Hemisphere star pattern is a great start for navigating the night sky, and it’s easier than you might think. 

Head to the link in bio to become an expert stargazer in just one week.

Photograph by Will Matsuda, National Geographic

posts.text:
When was the last time you thanked a bee? 
 
Bees look out for all of us—they pollinate crops and are responsible for a third of the food we eat—so it’s our responsibility to look out for the bees in return. Happy Earth Day!

Learn more about why we should bee grateful for bees by watching #SecretsOfTheBees, streaming now on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

There’s wonder above, below, and all around us. The salt flats of Bolivia blur the line between Earth and the cosmos. 🌍💫 #StepIntoWonder 

#WelcomeToEarthSeries is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

Getting to see bees up close is always exciting—but for @drsammygrams, watching how broomstick bees protect their food for the next generation was truly unforgettable.

#StepIntoWonder with National Geographic’s #EarthMonth collection throughout April and stream #SecretsOfTheBees on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

Presented by National Geographic Society in partnership with Rolex.

Antarctica might seem a frozen, lifeless place at first glance, but marine biologist and National Geographic Explorer Cristian Lagger is uncovering a very different story just offshore. On recent deep-sea expeditions in the Southern Ocean aboard the Schmidt Ocean Institute’s research vessel, Falkor (too), Lagger’s team has found thriving ecosystems deep below the surface—where icefish guard their eggs, octopuses drift through the currents, and colorful sponges and sea stars cover the seafloor.

Photo by Mattias Klum @mattiasklumofficial | On an expedition to the Galápagos Islands while working with the Charles Darwin Foundation, I witnessed something I had never seen in all my visits to the area. A juvenile red-footed booby was flying over me, tossing something awkwardly in its bill. Curious, I tracked it through my viewfinder. After a few frames I realized what it was wrestling with: a seahorse. The young bird, almost startled by its own discovery, dropped it. For a heartbeat, the two seemed to drift apart in the wind. It was a fleeting, delicate encounter I could never have imagined. Seabirds are sentinels of a changing ocean; they are indicators of warming waters, shifting currents, and fragile marine balances under pressure. Even after decades of work, I find that nature still reveals moments I could never script, reminding me that these islands will always have new stories to tell.

After breaking records with their recent lunar flyby, the Artemis II crew witnessed something truly extraordinary: a solar eclipse from the far side of the moon. Viewed using special eclipse glasses provided by NASA, the team is the first to witness a solar eclipse so close to the moon. From the crew’s perspective, the moon appeared large enough to completely block the sun, creating nearly 54 minutes of eclipse totality. The corona forms a glowing halo around the dark lunar disk, revealing details of the sun’s outer atmosphere typically hidden by its brightness. The faint glow of the nearside of the moon is visible in this image, having been illuminated by light reflected off the Earth. The astronauts also became the first humans to see the moon's Orientale basin, a nearly 600-mile-wide crater, visible in the bottom half of the second image.

"We will visit again," mission specialist Christina Koch said as the crew began its journey home. "We will construct science outposts. We will drive rovers. We will do radio astronomy. We will found companies. We will bolster industry. We will inspire but ultimately, we will always choose Earth. We will always choose each other."

See more photos from the lunar flyby at the link in bio. 

Photographs by @nasa

After flying farther beyond the Earth than any human has ever been before—beating the record set in 1970 during Apollo 13 by about 4,102 miles—and seeing parts of the moon no humans had ever witnessed, the Artemis II crew recreated “Earthrise,” one of the most famous photographs of all time, with a small twist. Instead of Earthrise, the new photo is of Earthset, capturing the surface of the moon and the crescent-lit Earth setting beyond it in the same frame. The dark portion of Earth is experiencing nighttime. On Earth’s day side, swirling clouds are visible over Australia and Oceania. In the foreground, Ohm crater on the moon has terraced edges and a flat floor interrupted by central peaks.

Photographs by @nasa

Photo by @beverlyjoubert | Places like Lake Magadi, in Kenya’s southern Rift Valley, remind us that the wild can be austere, caustic, almost otherworldly … and yet still profoundly alive. The smokelike effect you see on the water’s surface is caused by a combination of the lake’s unique mineral composition and a thin layer of mineral-rich brine that forms as water evaporates and is then stirred into motion by the wind. Beneath this shifting veil, life endures. Microscopic algae and bacteria thrive in the alkaline waters, forming the foundation of a food web that draws in vast flocks of flamingos, as well as other species uniquely adapted to survive here. In a landscape that appears too extreme to sustain anything at all, the smallest organisms create abundance and nature finds a way to persist.

Humanity is one small step closer to returning to the moon. At 6:35 pm EDT, the Artemis II mission blasted off from NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida atop the 300-plus foot-tall Space Launch System (SLS) rocket. The first crewed lunar mission in more than 50 years sends a team of four astronauts—Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, Mission Specialist Christina Koch, and Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen of the Canadian Space Agency—on a 10-day mission to slingshot around the moon and return to Earth. 
 
The mission features some notable firsts: Victor Glover will become the first Black man to visit the moon, and Christina Koch will become the first woman to visit the moon. While its astronauts will not land on the lunar surface, they’ll pave the way for future landings by testing new technologies designed for deep space exploration and taking part in pioneering experiments.

Coral reefs support a quarter of all marine life, but rising ocean temperatures are pushing many toward collapse. Marine biologist Anya Brown is exploring a surprising idea: that corals may help each other survive. By placing heat-tolerant colonies next to more vulnerable ones, she’s seen dramatic increases in survival.

Her research suggests corals may share beneficial microbes through their mucus-like skin. Now working from a makeshift lab in the Cook Islands, Brown is scaling her experiments to better understand how these relationships could help protect reefs.

Fluorescence is widespread in insects and arachnids, and photographer Javier Aznar González de Rueda (@javier_aznar_photography) uses ultraviolet light to reveal the beauty of these patterns. He's spent years tracking these creatures at night, adjusting his flashes to emit UV light and reveal details normally hidden in the dark. In doing so, he hopes his photos will help scientists understand what causes fluorescence. 

Head to the link in bio to explore his work and the details he's uncovered.

For decades, scientists have traced the origins of modern apes to East Africa. But a newly identified species—Masripithecus moghraensis, discovered in Egypt—may challenge that idea. The 17- to 18-million-year-old fossil suggests that the earliest ancestors of living apes were present farther north.

This 3D reconstruction of the ape is based on the jaw bones researchers found in Egypt. Learn how the discovery could upend our understanding of early ape evolution at the link in bio.

In Tahiti, freshwater doesn't just flow into rivers—it can also seep into the sea. Endometriosis is often considered a reproductive-related illness, but research now shows it can affect the whole body. Studies have linked the condition to a range of symptoms—from migraines and asthma to digestive disorders—highlighting a broader inflammatory impact. These findings are prompting experts to rethink how they diagnose and treat people living with the condition.

While scouring the seafloor for surprising creatures in Indonesia’s Lembeh Strait, photographer @ross_gudgeon came across a cluster of cauliflower soft coral. “Really boring from the outside,” Gudgeon says, since it simply resembles its namesake. In 2023, biologist and National Geographic Explorers David Gruber and Shane Gero set out to study sperm whales off Dominica's coast as part of a @projectceti team studying sperm whale communication. What they stumbled upon was far more extraordinary—a whale in labor.

For decades, whale births eluded observation. Project CETI's newly published findings provide a detailed picture not just of the birth, but of a whale family’s collaborative effort to care for the mother and infant—providing the first quantifiable evidence of an animal species other than primates engaging in an assisted birth.

Discover more about how sperm whale families support mothers in labor, and what it reveals about midwifery in the animal kingdom at the link in bio.

Anthropologist, primatologist, and National Geographic Explorer Dr. Biruté Galdikas has passed away at the age of 79. One of the so-called Trimates, alongside Dian Fossey and Jane Goodall, Galdikas spent thousands of hours in the Tanjung Puting Reserve in Borneo conducting the first long-term study of orangutans.
 
In an iconic 1975 National Geographic cover story, Galdikas shared her findings and experiences—radically changing how we understand these incredible primates. Photographed by Rod Brindamour, she holds hands and explores the reserve with orphaned orangutans, including Sugito who naps in an improvised nest made from an old dress and a hat that he swiped from Galdikas. "If we didn't provide rags," she reported, "the orangutans would bang around all night looking for nesting material."

Read more about Galdikas's legacy and her work with the National Geographic Society @insidenatgeo at the link in bio.

Photos by @joelsartore | It may look as though this Lewis’s moon snail, photographed @montereybayaquarium, is devouring itself, but don’t fret: There’s more to this image than meets the eye. These snails rarely stay inside their shells for long periods of time. Instead, they expand out of their shells to the point where it's hard to believe they ever fit inside to begin with. Much of the expansion is due to the uptake of water, which the snail will slowly release in order to contract back into its shell when it is disturbed and in need of shelter. Photo by Mattias Klum @mattiasklumofficial | The Jameson’s mamba is, in my opinion, one of the most stunning of all slithering creatures. The sleek, graceful snakes are usually 1.5 to 2.2 meters (4.9 to 7.2 feet) long. Predominantly tree dwellers, they feast mostly on birds. But don’t let their elegance fool you; Jameson’s mambas pack a punch. Their venom is a dangerous cocktail of hemotoxins, cardiotoxins, fasciculins, and myotoxins capable of killing within 30 to 120 minutes without treatment. Ironically, this deadly venom is also a lifesaver: It has inspired treatments for diabetes and Alzheimer’s disease. With some luck and hard work, Jameson’s mambas may soon help fight heart disease, proving that even the deadliest creatures have a role in nature—and in saving lives. I photographed this gorgeous snake in Cameroon on assignment for @natgeo.

See the images from our Photo of the Day collection. 📸

Photo 1: In this image captured by photographer @smitashrm, Akhil Singhal, 30, and Avani Gupta, 29, pose during their pre-wedding shoot in Kishangarh, Rajasthan, India. What may look like an ice cap is actually a depository for waste from the region's marble industry, a popular destination for social media influencers, pre-wedding photography, and tourists.

Photo 2: Captured by @christiehemmklok, a 10-year-old radiated tortoise named Mister escapes the heat in Pacifica, California. Given that they can live for over 150 years, the photo was featured in a December 2024 story about exotic pets that outlive their owners. These reptiles are native to Madagascar and are listed as critically endangered. 

Photo 3: In this image from our March 2026 issue, photographer Tiina Törmänen @tiinautti, who has spent years exploring and documenting lakes in remote northern Finland, captured European perch swimming through an algal bloom in Lapland. The bloom, likely caused by unusually warm summer waters linked to climate change, highlights small-scale shifts in the Arctic ecosystem. 

Photo 4: In this image shot by photographer @britta.jaschinski.photography, we see how a London Metropolitan Police forensic photographer used crime scene expertise to develop a process for revealing human fingerprints on porous elephant tusks, a new crime-fighting technique to stop the illegal wildlife trade.

Photo 5: Photographer @emmanuel.tardy caught this image of a brown-throated sloth clinging to a barbed wire fence post in El Tanque, Alajuela Province, Costa Rica. As their habitats become increasingly fragmented, these slow and discreet mammals are now often forced to cross open ground to survive.

Photo by @beverlyjoubert | Long necks etched against the sky in Kenya's Chyulu Hills: This is the giraffe silhouette we all know. But lying down? Rarely. Research shows that in the wild, giraffes, often standing and seldom fully at rest, take only a few minutes of deep sleep at a time, scattered across the night.  It's a rhythm shaped by survival: vigilance against predators, the need to move and feed, and life on the open savanna. And it certainly puts into perspective the luxury of a long, uninterrupted sleep we humans get to enjoy.
